0.name: Unknown / Unmapped Xid
0.likely_cause: Xid code not present in this reference table.
0.notes: Fallback entry. Consult NVIDIA's full Xid catalog (docs.nvidia.com/deploy/xid-errors) or nverror.h in open-gpu-kernel-modules for the definitive list.

6.name: Hardware Exception (MMU Fault)
6.likely_cause: Memory management unit exception on older GPU architectures (pre-Volta). Functionally equivalent to what Xid 31 reports on newer hardware.
6.notes: Largely superseded by Xid 31 on Volta and later. If seen on modern hardware, treat the same as Xid 31 — check for use-after-free or invalid pointer dereferences in CUDA code.

8.name: GPU Stopped Processing
8.likely_cause: Idle timeout on a FIFO channel.
8.notes: Rarely actionable on its own — diagnose the root cause Xid first. Appears alongside more specific errors as a secondary symptom.

11.name: Invalid/Corrupted Push Buffer Stream
11.likely_cause: Corrupted or invalid command stream sent to the GPU.
11.notes: Can be triggered by application bugs writing invalid GPU commands, or driver issues. If recurring without an obvious software cause, may point to a hardware problem with the command processor.

13.name: Graphics Engine Exception
13.likely_cause: Out-of-bounds array access, illegal instruction, or illegal register access within a kernel. Rarely a hardware fault.
13.notes: Run under compute-sanitizer (memcheck) or cuda-gdb, or set CUDA_DEVICE_WAITS_ON_EXCEPTION=1.

14.name: Fake/Injected Error
14.likely_cause: Injected error from userspace, not a real fault.
14.notes: Deprecated/unused on most modern setups; harmless if seen.

16.name: Display Engine Error
16.likely_cause: Display engine lost contact with the connected display or encountered a protocol error. Common during mode changes, hotplug events, or when running headless.
16.notes: Usually benign on compute/server nodes with no display attached. If a display is connected and this causes instability, check cable and display hardware.

25.name: Invalid/Illegal Push Buffer Stream
25.likely_cause: Illegal command stream sent to the GPU.
25.notes: Check application/CUDA usage if it recurs.

31.name: GPU Memory Page Fault
31.likely_cause: Use-after-free, allocator race condition (e.g. competing memory pools across PyTorch/CuPy/TensorRT), or DLPack zero-copy tensor lifetime bug.
31.notes: Run under compute-sanitizer (memcheck) or cuda-gdb, or set CUDA_DEVICE_WAITS_ON_EXCEPTION=1. Message includes faulting address, engine (GRAPHICS/COPY/NVDEC), fault type (PDE/PTE), and access type.

32.name: Invalid/Corrupted Push Buffer Stream (PBDMA)
32.likely_cause: PBDMA DMA controller fault managing driver-to-GPU communication over PCIe. Usually a PCIe quality issue rather than app-caused, but can also be triggered by application bugs writing invalid commands.
32.notes: Run the offending workload under compute-sanitizer to catch memory corruption early. If this appears without a user process running, suspect PCIe link quality or driver corruption.

38.name: Driver Firmware Error (FECS Watchdog)
38.likely_cause: FECS watchdog timeout; can also follow a GSP-RM fault or indicate a driver/firmware version mismatch.
38.notes: Try a clean driver reinstall if this appears after a driver upgrade. If it persists across driver versions on the same hardware, consider RMA. Contact support if recurring.

39.name: Copy Engine Exception
39.likely_cause: Fault on a GPU copy engine.

40.name: Copy Engine Exception
40.likely_cause: Fault on a GPU copy engine.

41.name: Copy Engine Exception
41.likely_cause: Fault on a GPU copy engine.

43.name: GPU Stopped Processing (Verif Error)
43.likely_cause: User application software-induced fault; GPU remains healthy.
43.notes: Usually a user app error, not a driver bug. Often appears alongside other Xids as a secondary symptom.

44.name: Graphics Engine Fault During Context Switch

45.name: Preemptive Channel Cleanup
45.likely_cause: Driver tore down a GPU app after abort (Ctrl-C, GPU reset, sigkill) or a preceding DBE.
45.notes: Often a user/system action, not a bug. Frequently seen alongside Xid 94/95.

48.name: Double Bit ECC Error (DBE)
48.likely_cause: Uncorrectable DRAM cell degradation; two bits flipped in the same memory word — ECC can detect but not correct.
48.notes: Corrupts any data in that location (e.g. training gradients); can cascade into Xid 31 if it hits page tables. GPU reset or node reboot required. Check nvidia-smi for SRAM DBE threshold; RMA if exceeded.

54.name: Auxiliary Power Not Connected
54.likely_cause: Auxiliary power cable unplugged or seated poorly.
54.notes: Visible directly via nvidia-smi power-cable status field.

56.name: Display Driver Error
56.likely_cause: nvidia-modeset (the display driver kernel module) encountered an error, often related to display engine state queries or mode changes.
56.notes: Commonly appears immediately after Xid 79 as a secondary effect — the display driver tries to query state from a lost GPU and fails. Not a root cause on its own.

61.name: Internal Micro-controller Error
61.likely_cause: An error occurred on the GPU's internal micro-controller (PMU / SEC2 / GSP depending on GPU generation).
61.notes: Often a precursor to or companion of Xid 62 (micro-controller halt). If repeated, try updating driver/firmware; if persistent, treat as potential hardware failure.

62.name: Internal Micro-controller Halt (PMU)
62.likely_cause: The GPU's PMU micro-controller stopped executing entirely — more severe than Xid 61 (error without halt).
62.notes: Full halt typically requires a GPU reset or node reboot to recover. If Xid 61 precedes this, treat 61 as the leading indicator to watch. Contact support if recurring.

63.name: ECC Row Remapping Pending
63.likely_cause: Memory row accumulated enough single-bit errors to require remapping; remap is pending a GPU reset.
63.notes: Row remap on Ampere+, replacing dynamic page retirement on pre-Ampere hardware. Not urgent by itself but schedule a GPU reset.

64.name: ECC Row Remapping Failure
64.likely_cause: Row remap could not be applied successfully, indicating deeper memory degradation.
64.notes: Escalate directly; this is a stronger signal than Xid 63.

65.name: Video Processor Exception (NVDEC/NVENC)
65.likely_cause: Exception in the video decode/encode processor, usually from invalid input data, unsupported codec parameters, or an application bug in the video pipeline.
65.notes: Affects NVDEC/NVENC workloads. Check the application's codec configuration and input stream validity. Unlikely to be hardware unless Xid 48 or 79 appear alongside.

68.name: NVDEC0 Exception
68.likely_cause: Hardware video decoder (NVDEC) encountered an error — typically from a malformed bitstream, unsupported decode configuration, or resource exhaustion.
68.notes: Validate the input video stream. NVDEC errors are almost always software or data issues rather than hardware faults, unless accompanied by ECC or bus errors.

73.name: NVENC Error
73.likely_cause: Hardware video encoder (NVENC) encountered an error — from an invalid encode configuration, unsupported resolution/codec combination, or too many concurrent encode sessions.
73.notes: Check NVENC session limits (varies by GPU SKU and driver version) and validate the encode parameters. Not a hardware fault indicator on its own.

74.name: NVLink Error
74.likely_cause: Link-level failure between GPUs or GPU-to-NVSwitch.
74.notes: GPU reset or node reboot needed. Use nvidia-smi nvlink for details. If a remote GPU fails, this GPU may report Xid 74 as a side effect. Cluster-level issue — investigate the fabric/switch, not just the single reporting GPU.

79.name: GPU Has Fallen Off the Bus
79.likely_cause: GPU became unresponsive on the PCIe bus, often due to power, thermal, seating, or hardware failure.
79.notes: Frequently requires a physical reseat or node reboot if reset doesn't recover it. Can also appear as multiline log entries without an explicit Xid tag.

80.name: Corrupted Data Sent to GPU
80.likely_cause: PBDMA pushbuffer CRC mismatch; PCIe-layer data corruption closely related to Xid 79.
80.notes: Check PCIe slot seating and power connectors. Often co-occurs with or precedes Xid 79.

92.name: High Single-Bit ECC Error Rate
92.likely_cause: Excessive but individually-correctable single-bit ECC errors.
92.notes: Monitor; contact support if rate keeps climbing.

93.name: InfoROM Erase Limit Exceeded
93.likely_cause: Driver failed to update InfoROM due to a wear-limit violation set intentionally via NVFlash.
93.notes: Usually intentional wear protection, not a failure. Recover via nvflash --elsessionclear.

94.name: Contained ECC Error
94.likely_cause: GPU error containment (A100+) kept the fault scoped to one app; other apps sharing the GPU were not affected.
94.notes: Restart the affected app; other apps on the GPU are unaffected. Reset GPU when convenient. Recurring Xid 94 is a strong signal to investigate the memory hardware. Often paired with Xid 45.

95.name: Uncontained Error / Multi-App Containment Failure
95.likely_cause: Same error family as Xid 94, but the fault affected multiple applications sharing the GPU.
95.notes: GPU must be reset before any new applications can run on it.

109.name: Context Switch Timeout
109.likely_cause: The GPU failed to complete a context switch within the expected time — typically caused by a stuck workload, runaway kernel, or an unresponsive GPU engine.
109.notes: Often the first visible sign that a GPU is becoming unresponsive before a full Xid 79. If Xid 109 precedes Xid 79 in the logs, treat 109 as the early warning indicator.

110.name: Security Fault Error
110.likely_cause: Uncommon unless there is a hardware failure.
110.notes: Revert recent hardware changes and cold-reset. Contact hardware vendor if it persists.

119.name: GSP RPC Timeout
119.likely_cause: GPU System Processor (GSP) failed to respond to a driver RPC call in time.
119.notes: Uncommon outside of hardware failure. If persistent, revert recent hardware changes and cold-reset the system. GPU reset or node power cycle may be needed.

120.name: GSP Error
120.likely_cause: An error occurred in code running on the GPU's GSP core.
120.notes: Often paired with Xid 119 in logs.

121.name: C2C Error
121.likely_cause: Corrected errors on the C2C NVLink connection to a Grace CPU.
121.notes: System-corrected, no operational impact. Reset GPU at a convenient window to retrain the link.

136.name: Link Training Failed
136.notes: Investigate link signal integrity if recurring.

137.name: NVLink Privilege Error
137.likely_cause: Illegal NVLink peer-to-peer access reported by the remote MMU.
137.notes: Typically an app-level bug; can be a driver/hardware bug. Debug like Xid 31.

140.name: ECC Unrecovered Error
140.likely_cause: Uncorrectable GPU memory errors interrupted the driver's ability to mark pages for offlining/remapping.
140.notes: Reset GPU; contact hardware vendor if it persists.

143.name: GSP Assert / Sanity Check Failure
143.likely_cause: An internal assertion or sanity check failed inside the GSP firmware, usually triggered by unexpected state from a prior error.
143.notes: Usually secondary noise after Xid 79 or 119 rather than a root cause. The assertion message in the log can help NVIDIA support pinpoint the firmware path that failed.

144.name: NVLink5 SAW Error
144.likely_cause: Fatal NVLink5-generation fabric error (SAW subcode) on B100/GB200 hardware.
144.notes: New error family (Xid 144-150) for NVLink5 hardware. Carries extended subcode/severity/link-number fields in the log line.

145.name: NVLink5 RLW Error
145.likely_cause: NVLink5-generation fabric error (RLW subcode) on B100/GB200 hardware.
145.notes: New error family (Xid 144-150) for NVLink5 hardware. Even individual events can degrade interconnect bandwidth; monitor frequency.

146.name: NVLink5 TLW Error
146.likely_cause: NVLink5-generation fabric error (TLW subcode) on B100/GB200 hardware.
146.notes: New error family (Xid 144-150) for NVLink5 hardware (B100/GB200).

147.name: NVLink5 TREX Error
147.likely_cause: NVLink5-generation fabric error (TREX subcode) on B100/GB200 hardware.
147.notes: New error family (Xid 144-150) for NVLink5 hardware (B100/GB200).

148.name: NVLink5 NVLPW_CTRL Error
148.likely_cause: NVLink5-generation fabric error (NVLPW_CTRL subcode) on B100/GB200 hardware.
148.notes: New error family (Xid 144-150) for NVLink5 hardware (B100/GB200).

149.name: NVLink5 NETIR Error
149.likely_cause: NVLink5-generation fabric error (NETIR subcode) on B100/GB200 hardware.
149.notes: New error family (Xid 144-150) for NVLink5 hardware (B100/GB200).

150.name: NVLink5 MSE Error
150.likely_cause: NVLink5-generation fabric error (MSE subcode) on B100/GB200 hardware.
150.notes: New error family (Xid 144-150) for NVLink5 hardware (B100/GB200).

154.name: GPU Recovery Action Required
154.likely_cause: Driver has determined a specific recovery action is needed for the GPU.
154.notes: Seen alongside other Xids; summarizes required recovery (None / Drain P2P / Drain and Reset / GPU Reset Required / Node Reboot Required). Visible via nvidia-smi's GPU Recovery Action field; pair with nvidia-smi output when parsing.

155.name: NVLink Software-Defined Error
155.likely_cause: Intentional link-down event (including transition to SLEEP).

156.name: Resource Retirement Event
156.likely_cause: A GPU resource was retired due to a fault.

157.name: Resource Retirement Failure
157.likely_cause: Resource retirement could not be applied due to lack of resources.
157.notes: No repair possible; workloads may still run but the Xid may recur.

158.name: GPU Fatal Timeout
158.likely_cause: Fatal timeout condition on the GPU.

159.name: CHI Non-Data Error
159.likely_cause: Non-data protocol error on a C2C-linked GPU.
159.notes: May be seen on any C2C link-connected GPU.

161.name: Channel Retirement Failure
161.likely_cause: Channel retirement could not be applied.

171.name: Uncorrectable DRAM Error
171.likely_cause: Supplementary detail to Xid 48, specifically identifying DRAM as the fault location.

172.name: Uncorrectable SRAM Error
172.likely_cause: Supplementary detail to Xid 48, specifically identifying SRAM as the fault location.
